passages.explanation: Luke anchors the story in world history with six time markers: the fifteenth year of Tiberius Caesar, Pontius Pilate governing Judea, Herod tetrarch of Galilee, Philip tetrarch of Iturea and Traconitis, Lysanias tetrarch of Abilene, and during the high-priesthood of Annas and Caiaphas. In this political context, the word of God comes — not to any of these powerful men, but to John son of Zechariah in the wilderness. John goes through the whole Jordan region, preaching a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins. Luke quotes Isaiah 40: a voice of one calling in the wilderness — prepare the way for the Lord, make straight paths for him. Every valley shall be filled in, every mountain made low. And all people will see God's salvation. Luke extends the Isaiah quote further than the other Gospels to include 'all people' — his universal theme.

John addresses the crowds coming for baptism with blunt force: you brood of vipers! Who warned you to flee from the coming wrath? Produce fruit in keeping with repentance. Don't say 'we have Abraham as our father' — God can raise children for Abraham from these stones. The axe is already at the root of the trees, and every tree that doesn't produce good fruit will be cut down and thrown into the fire. The crowd asks: what should we do? John's answers are practical, not theological. Anyone with two shirts should share with someone who has none; same with food. Tax collectors: don't collect more than required. Soldiers: don't extort money, don't accuse people falsely, be content with your pay. John doesn't tell them to quit their jobs — he tells them to do them honestly. Repentance has a shape, and it looks like justice.

The people are waiting expectantly and wondering if John might be the Messiah. John answers them all: I baptise you with water. But one who is more powerful than I will come — I'm not worthy to untie his sandals. He will baptise you with the Holy Spirit and fire. His winnowing fork is in his hand to clear his threshing floor and gather the wheat into his barn, but the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire. With many other words John exhorts the people and proclaims good news. John draws a hard line between himself and the one coming — the difference between water and fire, preparation and fulfilment.

John rebukes Herod the tetrarch for marrying Herodias, his brother's wife, and for all the other evil things Herod has done. Herod responds by locking John in prison — adding this sin to all his others. Luke mentions John's imprisonment here, before Jesus' baptism, wrapping up John's public ministry in a neat package before shifting focus entirely to Jesus.

When all the people are being baptised, Jesus is baptised too. As he prays, heaven opens. The Holy Spirit descends on him in bodily form like a dove. A voice comes from heaven: you are my Son, whom I love; with you I am well pleased. Luke emphasises that Jesus is praying — prayer at key moments is a major Lukan theme. The Trinity is present: the Father speaks, the Spirit descends, the Son is affirmed. Jesus' public ministry begins with his identity confirmed from heaven.

Luke inserts Jesus' genealogy here — after the baptism, when Jesus is about thirty years old. Unlike Matthew's genealogy (which starts with Abraham and moves forward), Luke traces the line backwards from Jesus through Joseph, through David, through Abraham, all the way back to Adam, the son of God. The genealogy differs from Matthew's in several places, likely tracing a different family line. Luke's point is theological: by going back to Adam, he shows Jesus as the saviour not just of Israel but of all humanity. The son of Adam, the son of God — the new beginning for the whole human race.
